doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: ABSTRACT
text:
This paper presents a compact three-class sentiment analysis study for Indonesian social media text. The task is formulated with positive , negative , and neutral outputs derived from a fine-grained emotion dataset. The proposed practical baseline combines TF-IDF text features, three lightweight numeric metadata features, and a balanced multinomial Logistic Regression classifier. For comparison, the study also includes a neural baseline using a two-layer multilayer perceptron (MLP) over the same hybrid feature representation. The dataset originally contains 732 rows and 191 fine-grained emotion labels; after cleaning, deduplication, and label remapping, 707 samples remain with an imbalanced distribution of 459 positive, 188 negative, and 60 neutral instances. Experimental results show that the Logistic Regression deployment model reaches 0.8028 accuracy, 0.8003 weighted F1, and 0.7276 macro F1, while project documentation reports a higher-accuracy but non-production MLP baseline. These findings indicate that careful preprocessing, interpretable feature engineering, and class balancing remain competitive for small Indonesian sentiment datasets, whereas the neural baseline is better treated as a comparative experiment than as the default deployment model.
Keywords Indonesian NLP · Sentiment Analysis · Logistic Regression · MLPClassifier · TF-IDF · Social Media Text

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 1 Introduction
text:
Sentiment analysis is one of the most established text classification tasks in natural language processing because it converts user opinions into structured signals for monitoring public response, customer satisfaction, and social trends [Pang and Lee, 2008, Medhat et al., 2014]. In practice, Indonesian sentiment analysis remains challenging because user-generated text is noisy, code-mixed, abbreviated, and often semantically ambiguous. These characteristics are particularly visible in social media data, where informal spelling, slang, emotive words, hashtags, mentions, and short context windows make polarity detection harder than in clean benchmark corpora.
Many recent sentiment-analysis papers emphasize large neural or transformer models, but small and noisy datasets do not always benefit equally from that additional modeling complexity. In low-resource settings, the more immediate challenge is often to design a reproducible pipeline that handles label ambiguity, class imbalance, and deployment constraints without sacrificing interpretability. The specific contribution of this study is therefore not a new model architecture, but a carefully documented comparison between deployable classical and lightweight neural baselines under the same hybrid feature space for Indonesian social media sentiment classification.
The system studied in this work adopts a production-first perspective. Instead of directly optimizing for the most complex architecture, it prioritizes reproducible preprocessing, stable deployment, and interpretable hybrid features. The production model combines TF-IDF unigram-bigram features with three numeric features derived from the cleaned text and metadata: text length in words, total engagement, and hashtag count. The deep learning branch is preserved as a separate experimental baseline implemented with an MLP over the same feature space.
A second challenge addressed by the repository is label granularity. The original dataset contains many emotion labels such as joy , nostalgia , grief , surprise , and curiosity . The pipeline remaps these fine-grained labels into the operational labels positive , negative , and neutral . This remapping is necessary for deployment, but it also introduces semantic compression and ambiguity, especially for mixed labels such as bittersweet , sympathy , and surprise . Because of this issue, weighted F1 alone is not sufficient; macro F1 and class-level discussion are also needed.
Based on the available implementation artifacts and model reports, this paper makes three contributions. First, it formalizes a reproducible end-to-end workflow for Indonesian three-class sentiment analysis, from cleaning and label remapping to deployment-ready inference. Second, it evaluates a hybrid classical baseline and a shallow neural baseline under the same input representation, enabling a more controlled comparison of classifier families. Third, it highlights the methodological implications of label compression, data imbalance, and incomplete benchmark preservation for small-scale sentiment-analysis studies.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 2 Related Work
text:
Classical sentiment analysis has long relied on lexical features, bag-of-words statistics, and linear classifiers because they provide strong baselines and interpretable decision boundaries [Pang et al., 2002, Joachims, 1998]. TF-IDF combined with Logistic Regression remains competitive for low-resource or medium-sized text classification tasks, especially when the dataset is not large enough to justify heavy neural models [Joachims, 1998, Zhang et al., 2010].
Recent Indonesian sentiment analysis studies have explored both conventional machine learning and deep learning strategies, especially for noisy user-generated text. Across this literature, preprocessing choices, feature representation, and class balance strongly influence final performance [Medhat et al., 2014, Alzahrani et al., 2023]. For Indonesian and multilingual social media text, normalization of slang, non-standard spelling, and noisy punctuation is particularly important because informal writing patterns can dominate token statistics. This study positions itself in that line of work by emphasizing reproducibility and deployment-oriented comparison rather than only raw predictive performance.
Distributed representations and deep neural networks improve text classification by learning dense feature interactions beyond sparse lexical counts [Mikolov et al., 2013, Kim, 2014]. Recurrent models such as LSTM can capture longer contextual dependencies [Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997], while attention-based transformers such as BERT provide contextualized embeddings that are now dominant across many NLP tasks [Devlin et al., 2019]. Indonesian-specific pretrained models such as IndoBERT further improve local-language performance by adapting transformer pretraining to Indonesian corpora [Koto et al., 2020].
However, strong deep models usually require larger training corpora, more compute, and more complex deployment pipelines. For compact educational or production-ready systems, lightweight neural baselines such as multilayer perceptrons still provide a useful midpoint between classical sparse models and full transformer stacks [Goodfellow et al., 2016]. This repository adopts that pragmatic view: Logistic Regression is maintained as the production model, while an MLP baseline is retained for comparative experimentation. The design aligns with the broader literature showing that simpler models can remain attractive under limited-data, limited-resource, and maintainability constraints [Sculley et al., 2015, Lipton, 2018].

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 3.1 Dataset Characteristics
text:
The study uses a social-media sentiment dataset stored in project-ml/data/raw/sentimentdataset.csv . The raw file contains 732 rows and 191 unique fine-grained sentiment labels. After cleaning, removing empty text, and dropping duplicates, the effective dataset contains 707 samples. The final class distribution is 459 positive, 188 negative, and 60 neutral samples, indicating a strong imbalance toward positive sentiment.
Table 1 summarizes the dataset characteristics most relevant to the experimental design. The strong skew in the class distribution is one of the main reasons for reporting macro F1 in addition to accuracy and weighted F1.
Figure 1 visualizes this imbalance directly. The positive class is several times larger than the neutral class, which helps explain why weighted metrics remain relatively high even when minority-class predictions are less stable.

figures.caption: Figure 1: Class distribution after preprocessing. The positive class dominates the dataset, while the neutral class remains the smallest category, which helps explain the gap between weighted and macro-level performance.
figures.description:
Here's an analysis of the image based on its visual content:

The image is a bar chart presented within a document titled "Class Distribution After Cleaning." The chart displays the distribution of sentiment classes across a dataset.

**Chart Elements:**

*   **Title:** "Class Distribution After Cleaning"
*   **X-Axis Label:** "Sentiment Class"
*   **Y-Axis Label:** "Number of Samples"
*   **Bar 1 (Positive):** A green bar with the label "positive" and a value of 459.
*   **Bar 2 (Negative):** A red bar with the label "negative" and a value of 188.
*   **Bar 3 (Neutral):** An orange bar with the label "neutral" and a value of 60.

**Data Points:**

The chart represents three distinct sentiment classes: positive, negative, and neutral. The numerical values associated with each class indicate the number of samples belonging to that category. Specifically:

*   459 samples are classified as "positive."
*   188 samples are classified as "negative."
*   60 samples are classified as "neutral."

**Trends & Patterns:**

The chart demonstrates a clear imbalance in sentiment distribution, with the "positive" class significantly outnumbering the other two. The bar lengths visually represent this disparity.

tables.markdown:
Table 1: Dataset summary after preprocessing.

| Attribute                                  | Value                                                             |
|--------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Raw samples                                | 732                                                               |
| Samples after cleaning and deduplica- tion | 707                                                               |
| Original label space                       | 191 fine-grained emotion labels                                   |
| Operational label space                    | 3 classes: positive, negative, neutral                            |
| Class distribution                         | 459 positive, 188 negative, 60 neutral                            |
| Main data challenge                        | Severe class imbalance and semantically ambiguous label remapping |
tables.caption: Table 1: Dataset summary after preprocessing.
tables.summary:
Here's a summary of the table's structure and the information it presents:

**Structure:**

The table is a simple data table with two columns:

*   **Attribute:** This column lists different characteristics or properties related to a dataset (likely for emotion recognition).
*   **Value:** This column provides the corresponding value for each attribute.

**Key Information Represented:**

The table describes several aspects of a dataset used for emotion recognition, specifically focusing on its size and challenges:

*   **Sample Size:** The dataset started with 732 raw samples, but after cleaning and deduplication, it was reduced to 707 samples.
*   **Labeling:** There were initially 191 fine-grained emotion labels (meaning a detailed categorization of emotions), which were then narrowed down to 3 classes: positive, negative, and neutral.
*   **Class Distribution:** The distribution of these classes is significantly imbalanced: 459 are positive, 188 are negative, and only 60 are neutral.
*   **Main Data Challenge:**  The primary difficulty identified is a "severe class imbalance and semantically ambiguous label remapping," indicating that the dataset's unequal distribution of emotions makes it difficult to train accurate models, and the labels themselves might be somewhat unclear or overlapping.

In essence, this table outlines the characteristics of a dataset designed for emotion recognition, highlighting its size, labeling scheme, and significant class imbalance - all factors that would need careful consideration when developing machine learning models for this task.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 3.2 Preprocessing Pipeline
text:
The preprocessing pipeline is directly reflected in the implementation. Text is lowercased, URLs and user mentions are removed, and simple leetspeak normalization is applied. The code also expands common Indonesian slang variants, for example mapping gak to tidak and bgt to banget . After that, non-alphabetic characters are discarded, repeated spaces are collapsed, and empty texts are removed. This sequence is intentionally lightweight so that the resulting system remains easy to reproduce and deploy.
The label preprocessing stage maps 191 raw emotion labels into three operational categories. Positive labels include emotions such as joy , gratitude , and serenity ; negative labels include anger , grief , and frustration ; neutral labels include curiosity , confusion , and indifference . Labels such as surprise , nostalgia , and bittersweet remain semantically ambiguous after compression into three classes. This label-mapping step is operationally useful, but it also introduces a source of noise that should be considered when interpreting the evaluation results.
Table 2 gives a compact example of the label-mapping logic used in the preprocessing stage. The mapping examples are consistent with the exported summary table stored in paper_outputs/label_mapping_mini_table.csv .
Besides TF-IDF text vectors, the code creates three lightweight numeric features: the number of words in the cleaned text, total engagement computed from retweets plus likes, and the number of hashtags. These features are standardized and concatenated with the TF-IDF representation to form the final hybrid input vector.

tables.markdown:
Table 2: Mini example of fine-grained label remapping into three sentiment classes.

| Raw emotion label                                                         | Mapped class                                                                  |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Joy Gratitude Excitement Sad Anger Frustrated Neutral Confusion Curiosity | positive positive positive negative negative negative neutral neutral neutral |
tables.caption: Table 2: Mini example of fine-grained label remapping into three sentiment classes.
tables.summary:
Here's a summary of the table's structure and the information it represents:

**Structure:**

The table is a simple two-column data structure. It has two headings:

*   **Raw emotion label:** This column lists specific words representing different emotions (e.g., Joy, Anger, Sad).
*   **Mapped class:** This column assigns each raw emotion to one of three categories: "positive," "negative," or "neutral."

**Key Information:**

The table demonstrates a mapping system for classifying emotions. It provides a way to categorize emotional expressions based on their overall sentiment.  Essentially, it's defining how certain words are categorized into broader emotional groups. 

*   **Positive Emotions:** Joy, Gratitude, and Excitement are all classified as positive emotions.
*   **Negative Emotions:** Anger and Frustration are classified as negative emotions.
*   **Neutral Emotions:** Sadness, Confusion, and Curiosity are classified as neutral emotions.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 4.1 Classical ML Pipeline
text:
The production pipeline follows a straightforward hybrid architecture. First, the cleaned Indonesian text is transformed using TF-IDF with up to 3,000 features, min_df =2, max_df =0.9, unigram-bigram range, and sublinear term frequency scaling. Second, the numeric metadata features are normalized with StandardScaler . Third, the sparse text features and dense numeric features are concatenated. Finally, a multinomial Logistic Regression classifier with class_weight=balanced , C =2.0, lbfgs solver, and max_iter =2000 is trained on an 80:20 stratified split.
This design is suitable for small datasets because linear models handle high-dimensional sparse vectors efficiently while balanced class weighting partially compensates for the dataset skew. The pipeline also preserves probabilistic outputs and lightweight deployment artifacts, which is important for a Hugging Face Space or small local application.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 4.2 Neural Baseline Architecture
text:
The repository keeps a separate neural baseline implemented with MLPClassifier . Although the folder is named deep learning , the actual baseline is a feed-forward neural network rather than a sequence model. The architecture has two hidden layers with sizes (256 , 64) , ReLU activation, Adam optimization, weight decay alpha =10 -4 , learning rate initialization of 10 -3 , early stopping, and a maximum of 60 iterations.
The key point is that the MLP receives the same TF-IDF plus numeric hybrid feature vector as the classical pipeline. Therefore, the comparison isolates the classifier family rather than changing the full input representation. In other words, the experiment compares a linear model against a shallow neural architecture under the same preprocessing regime.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 5.1 Experimental Setup
text:
All experiments use a stratified train-test split with 80% training data and 20% test data, with random_state =42. In the preserved evaluation artifact, this corresponds to 565 training samples and 142 test samples. The operational target is three-class sentiment classification. The main evaluation metrics are accuracy, weighted F1, and macro F1. Accuracy is useful for overall correctness, weighted F1 reflects performance under imbalance, and macro F1 better captures minority-class behavior. Because the dataset is small and imbalanced, the reported scores should be interpreted as point estimates from a single split rather than as definitive generalization bounds.
The classical production configuration uses TF-IDF with 3,000 maximum features, word unigrams and bigrams, and standardized numeric features. The neural baseline uses the same hybrid input space, making the comparison more controlled. The development notebook also indicates an AutoML-style screening stage using PyCaret to compare multiple classical ML candidates, including Logistic Regression, KNN, Naive Bayes, Decision Tree, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, AdaBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, and Extra Trees. However, only a subset of these comparisons is preserved as final reportable artifacts.

heading: 5.2 Hyperparameters
text: The main hyperparameters are summarized in Table 3, following the values exported in paper_outputs/hyperparameter_table.csv . These settings show that both the classical and neural baselines remain lightweight and reproducible.

tables.markdown:
Table 3: Main hyperparameters used in the preserved experiments.

| Component                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | Hyperparameter                                                                                                                                                                        | Value                                                                                         |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Logistic Regression Logistic Regression Logistic Regression Logistic Regression Logistic Regression MLPClassifier MLPClassifier MLPClassifier MLPClassifier MLPClassifier MLPClassifier MLPClassifier TF-IDF TF-IDF TF-IDF TF-IDF TF-IDF | max_iter class_weight solver C random_state hidden_layer_sizes activation solver alpha learning_rate_init max_iter early_stopping max_features min_df max_df ngram_range sublinear_tf | 2000 balanced lbfgs 2.0 42 (256 , 64) relu adam 10 - 4 10 - 3 60 True 3000 2 0.9 (1 , 2) True |
tables.caption: Table 3: Main hyperparameters used in the preserved experiments.
tables.summary:
Here's a summary of the table's structure and the information it represents:

**Structure:**

The table is organized into two columns:

*   **Component:** This column lists different machine learning components or models being configured (Logistic Regression, MLPClassifier, TF-IDF).
*   **Hyperparameter:** This column lists the specific hyperparameters associated with each component.  A hyperparameter is a setting that controls the learning process of a model - it's not learned from data itself but set by the user.

**Key Information Represented:**

The table shows the configuration settings used for several machine learning models:

*   **Logistic Regression:** It specifies hyperparameters like `max_iter` (maximum number of iterations), `class_weight` (balanced - indicating class imbalance handling), and `solver` (IbPGS - Interior Point Gradient Stabilization).
*   **MLPClassifier (Multi-Layer Perceptron Classifier):**  It defines parameters for a neural network, including `hidden_layer_sizes` (the size of the hidden layers in the network), `activation` (relu - Rectified Linear Unit activation function), and `solver` (adam - Adaptive Moment Estimation optimization algorithm).
*   **TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency):** It sets parameters for text processing, including `max_features` (maximum number of features to consider), `min_df` (minimum document frequency), `max_df` (maximum document frequency), `ngram_range` (the range of n-grams to use), and `sublinear_tf` (whether to use sublinear term weighting).

In essence, the table is a configuration file or settings list for training these machine learning models.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 5.3 Benchmark Table
text: Table 4 compares the preserved benchmark entries exported in paper_outputs/model_benchmark_table.csv . In contrast to the earlier draft, the current table now reports concrete metrics for four models: Linear SVM, MLPClassifier, Logistic Regression, and Random Forest.

tables.markdown:
Table 4: Benchmark comparison between three classical models and one neural baseline.

| Model               | Family            |   Accuracy |   Macro F1 |   Weighted F1 |
|---------------------|-------------------|------------|------------|---------------|
| Linear SVM          | Classical ML      |     0.8521 |     0.7553 |        0.8476 |
| MLPClassifier       | Neural base- line |     0.8451 |     0.7263 |        0.8304 |
| Logistic Regression | Classical ML      |     0.8028 |     0.7276 |        0.8003 |
| Random Forest       | Classical ML      |     0.7324 |     0.4821 |        0.6749 |
tables.caption: Table 4: Benchmark comparison between three classical models and one neural baseline.
tables.summary:
Here's a summary of the table's structure and the information it presents:

**Structure:**

The table is organized into rows, with each row representing a different machine learning model.  It has three columns:

*   **Model:** Lists the name of the specific machine learning model being evaluated (e.g., Linear SVM, MLPClassifier, Logistic Regression, Random Forest).
*   **Family:** Indicates the broader category or "family" to which the model belongs (e.g., Classical ML, Neural base-line).
*   **Accuracy, Macro F1, Weighted F1:** These columns display performance metrics for each model.

    *   **Accuracy:** The overall percentage of correct predictions made by the model.
    *   **Macro F1:**  A metric that averages the F1 scores across all classes, giving equal weight to each class regardless of its frequency.
    *   **Weighted F1:** A metric that calculates the F1 score for each class and then averages them, weighting the scores by the number of true instances in each class.

**Key Information Represented:**

The table compares the performance of four different machine learning models (Linear SVM, MLPClassifier, Logistic Regression, and Random Forest) based on three key metrics: Accuracy, Macro F1, and Weighted F1.  It shows how well each model performs in terms of correctly classifying data points. 

**Key Observations from the Table:**

*   The MLPClassifier (Neural base-line) consistently achieves the highest accuracy (0.8451) and weighted F1 score (0.8304).
*   The Linear SVM has a high weighted F1 score of 0.8476, indicating good overall performance.
*   Logistic Regression performs reasonably well with an accuracy of 0.8028 and a weighted F1 score of 0.8003.
*   The Random Forest model has the lowest accuracy (0.7324) and weighted F1 score (0.6749) among the models presented, suggesting it might not be as effective in this particular dataset or task.

Let me know if you'd like a deeper dive into any specific aspect of the table!

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 6 Results and Discussion
text:
The main deployment-focused result from the saved evaluation artifact is that Logistic Regression achieves 0.8028 accuracy, 0.8003 weighted F1, and 0.7276 macro F1 on the held-out test set. These values indicate that the model performs solidly overall while still facing difficulty on the minority classes. In particular, the positive class is the easiest to detect, whereas the neutral class remains the hardest because it has the fewest instances and the most ambiguous semantics after label compression.
Table 5 reports the class-wise metrics exported in paper_outputs/per_class_metrics_table.csv . The positive class achieves the highest F1-score, whereas the neutral class remains the most difficult category.
The project documentation also reports that the MLP baseline attains 0.8451 accuracy, 0.8304 weighted F1, and 0.7263 macro F1. This means that the neural baseline improves overall accuracy and weighted F1, but does not clearly surpass the Logistic Regression deployment model in macro F1. This is an important observation: on a small and imbalanced dataset, gains in majority-class performance can lift accuracy without substantially solving minority-class ambiguity.
From a deployment perspective, keeping Logistic Regression as the default production model is reasonable. The model is easier to serialize, simpler to interpret, faster to retrain, and less demanding operationally than a neural model. Even though Linear SVM attains the best preserved benchmark score, the current study prioritizes a model choice that is already aligned with the existing application pipeline and produces stable probabilistic outputs. The study therefore supports a pragmatic trade-off: the neural baseline is useful for experimentation, but Logistic Regression remains preferable for a stable production-first workflow.
The exported benchmark reveals an important methodological trade-off. Linear SVM records the highest overall accuracy and macro F1 among the preserved models, whereas Logistic Regression remains the chosen production model because of its deployment simplicity, stable probabilistic behavior, and readiness for the existing application pipeline. Random Forest performs substantially worse, especially in macro F1, which supports the decision to avoid tree-based majority-class bias in this imbalanced setting.

tables.markdown:
Table 5: Class-wise test metrics of the Logistic Regression model.

| Class    |   Precision |   Recall |   F1-score |   Support |
|----------|-------------|----------|------------|-----------|
| negative |      0.7429 |   0.6842 |     0.7123 |        38 |
| neutral  |      0.6364 |   0.5833 |     0.6087 |        12 |
| positive |      0.8438 |   0.8804 |     0.8617 |        92 |
tables.caption: Table 5: Class-wise test metrics of the Logistic Regression model.
tables.summary:
Here's a summary of the table's structure and the information it represents:

**Structure:**

The table is a confusion matrix, a standard tool used to evaluate the performance of classification models (like sentiment analysis). It shows how well a model predicts different classes.

*   **Rows:** Represent the *actual* or true class labels. In this case, there are three classes: "negative," "neutral," and "positive."
*   **Columns:** Represent the *predicted* class labels made by the model.
*   **Cells:** Each cell contains a numerical value representing the count of instances that fall into that specific combination of true and predicted class.

**Key Information:**

The table provides several metrics for evaluating the classification model's performance:

*   **Precision:**  For each class, this shows the proportion of correctly predicted positive instances out of all instances predicted as positive. (e.g., For "positive," 84.38% of predictions labeled as "positive" were actually positive.)
*   **Recall:** For each class, this shows the proportion of correctly predicted positive instances out of all actual positive instances. (e.g., For "negative," 68.42% of all negative instances were correctly identified).
*   **F1-score:** This is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a balanced measure of accuracy.  A higher F1-score indicates a better balance between precision and recall. (e.g., For "positive," the F1-score is 0.8617).
*   **Support:** This shows the number of actual instances belonging to each class in the dataset. This helps understand the prevalence of each class. (e.g., "Positive" has a support of 92, meaning there were 92 actual positive examples in the dataset.)

**In essence, this table tells us how accurately the model is classifying sentiment into three categories.**  The higher the precision, recall, and F1-score for each class, the better the model performs. The "Support" value indicates the size of each class within the training data.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 6.1 Error Analysis
text:
The exported class-wise report confirms that most residual errors concentrate in the neutral category. This behavior is consistent with the dataset distribution and with the semantic ambiguity introduced by the three-class remapping. Neutral examples are likely to overlap lexically with both positive and negative posts, especially when the original fine-grained label expresses mixed affect, mild surprise, curiosity, or context-dependent reactions. As a result, a model can achieve strong weighted performance while still making unstable predictions on minority or ambiguous instances.
The comparison between Logistic Regression and MLPClassifier further supports this interpretation. The MLP improves accuracy and weighted F1, yet its macro F1 remains close to that of Logistic Regression. The benchmark figure also shows that Linear SVM performs best among the preserved models, but the margin between SVM and MLP remains smaller than the gap between these models and Random Forest. This pattern suggests that the main unresolved challenge is not only classifier capacity, but also the structure of the label space and the limited amount of neutral training data.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 6.2 Threats to Validity
text: Several factors limit the strength of the conclusions. First, the dataset is relatively small, so a single train-test split may not fully reflect performance variability. Second, the mapping from 191 fine-grained emotions to three sentiment classes is inherently subjective for some labels, which introduces annotation noise at the target level. Third, the benchmark table only reflects the preserved subset of experiments available in the current project outputs, so it may not represent the full search space originally explored during development. Finally, although the manuscript now includes class-wise tables and a confusion matrix, it still lacks representative qualitative examples of misclassified texts.

figures.caption: Figure 3: Benchmark comparison exported from paper_outputs/model_benchmark_bar_chart.png . Linear SVM yields the best overall preserved performance, while Random Forest performs worst in macro F1.

Here's an analysis of the image based on its visual content:

**Image Description:**

The image is a bar chart presented within a document, likely a scientific report or technical paper. The chart's title is "Benchmark Comparison Across Models".  It compares four machine learning models against two metrics: Accuracy and Macro F1 score.

**Data Points & Trends:**

*   **X-axis Labels:** The x-axis labels identify the following models: "Linear SVM", "MLPClassifier Model", "Logistic Regression", and "Random Forest".
*   **Y-axis Label:** The y-axis is labeled "Score" ranging from 0.0 to 1.0.
*   **Blue Bars (Accuracy):**
    *   Linear SVM: Score = 0.862
    *   MLPClassifier Model: Score = 0.860
    *   Logistic Regression: Score = 0.803
    *   Random Forest: Score = 0.640
*   **Orange Bars (Macro F1):**
    *   Linear SVM: Score = 0.715
    *   MLPClassifier Model: Score = 0.709
    *   Logistic Regression: Score = 0.803
    *   Random Forest: Score = 0.640

**Overall Pattern:**

The blue bars (Accuracy) consistently show higher scores than the orange bars (Macro F1) across all four models.  Linear SVM and MLPClassifier Model have the highest accuracy scores, while Random Forest has the lowest score for both metrics. Logistic Regression's accuracy is slightly lower than Linear SVM and MLPClassifier Model but comparable to their Macro F1 scores.

figures.caption: Figure 4: Confusion matrix of the Logistic Regression model exported in paper_outputs . The positive class is detected most reliably, while neutral remains the most error-prone category.
figures.description:
Here's an analysis of the image based on its visual content:

**Image Description:**

The image is a confusion matrix representing the performance of a logistic regression model. It's presented as a square grid with four cells arranged in two rows and two columns. The title of the matrix is "Confusion Matrix - Logistic Regression".

**Cell Values:**

*   **Top Left Cell:** Value 26
*   **Top Middle Cell:** Value 0
*   **Top Right Cell:** Value 12
*   **Bottom Left Cell:** Value 7
*   **Bottom Middle Cell:** Value 4
*   **Bottom Right Cell:** Value 81

**Axes Labels:**

*   **Horizontal Axis (Predicted Label):**  Displays the predicted class labels: "negative", "neutral", and "positive".
*   **Vertical Axis (True Label):** Displays the true class labels: "negative", "neutral", and "positive".

**Color Coding:**

The cells are colored with varying shades of blue, ranging from light to dark. The darker shades represent higher counts.

**Data Summary:**

*   **Negative Predicted, Negative True:** 26 instances
*   **Negative Predicted, Neutral True:** 0 instances
*   **Negative Predicted, Positive True:** 12 instances
*   **Neutral Predicted, Negative True:** 7 instances
*   **Neutral Predicted, Neutral True:** 4 instances
*   **Neutral Predicted, Positive True:** 81 instances

**Overall Structure:**

The matrix visually represents the classification accuracy of the logistic regression model. It shows the number of times the model correctly (true positive and true negative) or incorrectly (false positive and false negative) predicted each class label based on the actual (true) labels of the data.

doc_id: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1]
filename: Hybrid TF--IDF Logistic Regression and MLP Neural Baseline for Indonesian Three-Class Sentiment Analysis on Social Media Text [arXiv 2605.07793v1].pdf
heading: 7 Conclusion
text:
This paper presents a concise research-style report on Indonesian sentiment analysis using a compact hybrid feature pipeline. The implementation combines TF-IDF text features, lightweight metadata features, and balanced Logistic Regression for production use, while preserving an MLP-based neural baseline for experimentation. The dataset is small, label-rich, and imbalanced, which makes preprocessing quality and label remapping especially important.
The main takeaway is that a carefully engineered classical pipeline remains highly effective for compact Indonesian sentiment analysis tasks, especially when deployment simplicity and interpretability matter. At the same time, the preserved benchmark suggests that stronger alternatives remain possible, both from lightweight neural models and from competitive classical baselines such as Linear SVM. Future work should report repeated or cross-validated evaluation, add qualitative error examples, test stronger contextual models such as LSTM or IndoBERT, enlarge the neutral class, and preserve a complete benchmark export for all candidate models.
